value.report_date_session: normal Thursday global session dominated by the 8:30 a.m. EDT May PCE release and a renewed Strait of Hormuz supply scare
value.comparison_basis: US rate moves measured vs Wednesday 2026-06-24; Trading Economics commodity and FX moves are day-over-day CFD changes
dominant_drivers: May PCE printed firm year-over-year (headline 4.1 percent, core 3.4 percent) but in line to a touch soft on the monthly headline (0.4 percent)
a cargo-ship strike off the Omani coast re-introduced a Strait of Hormuz risk premium, reversing oil higher and lifting gold
value.relation_to_prior: a partial unwind of June 24's oil-collapse disinflation trade

text: June 25, 2026 was a normal Thursday global macro session, framed by two events: the 8:30 a.m. EDT release of the May PCE price index (the Fed's preferred inflation gauge) and a renewed Strait of Hormuz supply scare after a cargo ship was struck off the Omani coast. US rate moves are measured against the prior session of Wednesday, June 24 - the official Treasury par-yield CSV read live has its newest row dated 06/25/2026 directly after the 06/24/2026 row - while the Trading Economics commodity and FX figures are day-over-day CFD changes. The day was two-sided: realized inflation confirmed firm (headline PCE 4.1 percent year over year, core 3.4 percent) yet the in-line monthly print let Treasuries rally modestly, while the geopolitical risk premium came back into oil and gold - a partial unwind of June 24's oil-collapse disinflation trade.

value.shape: marginal bull steepening; broadly flat with the front falling slightly more than the long end
value.ten_year_low_marker: 4.40 percent is the lowest 10Y par yield since 2026-05-08 (4.38); every session from 05/11 through 06/24 was at or above 4.41

calculation.method: read official Treasury par-yield CSV live; 10Y minus 2Y; change vs the 06/24/2026 row; historical low verified against the May 2026 CSV

text: US Treasury par yields eased modestly on June 25, closing at 4.09 percent (2Y), 4.13 percent (3Y), 4.15 percent (5Y), 4.26 percent (7Y), 4.40 percent (10Y), 4.87 percent (20Y) and 4.86 percent (30Y) - down about 1 to 2 bp across the front and belly versus the June 24 close (2Y 4.11, 10Y 4.41) and flat at the long end. With the 2-year falling 2 bp and the 10-year 1 bp, the 2s10s slope on the official constant-maturity par basis nudged up 1 bp to +31 bp. The 10-year at 4.40 percent is its lowest since May 8, 2026 (4.38), having held at or above 4.41 percent on every session from May 11 through June 24. The long end kept its small kink, with the 20-year (4.87) one basis point above the 30-year (4.86). The modest rally came even as PCE confirmed firm inflation, because the monthly headline landed in line with expectations.

value.headline_vs_consensus: the monthly headline of 0.4 percent landed in line to about 0.1 pp below the 0.5 percent consensus previewed on June 24
value.significance: confirms inflation at a multi-year-high trajectory and core well above the Fed's 2 percent target, yet the in-line monthly print let Treasuries rally modestly

calculation.method: BEA official Personal Income and Outlays release for May 2026 (BEA 26-31); year-over-year and month-over-month as published

text: The day's headline data was the May PCE price index, released by the BEA at 8:30 a.m. EDT. The Fed's preferred gauge rose 0.4 percent on the month and 4.1 percent from a year earlier, while core PCE (excluding food and energy) rose 0.3 percent on the month and 3.4 percent year over year - core remaining far above the Fed's 2 percent target. BEA's headline year-over-year series has climbed steeply from 2.9 percent in February to 3.5 percent in March, 3.8 percent in April and 4.1 percent in May. Personal income rose 0.7 percent and real PCE was unchanged. Crucially for markets, the monthly headline of 0.4 percent came in line to about a tenth below the 0.5 percent consensus previewed the day before, which is why Treasuries could rally even on a firm annual inflation reading.

value.brent_low_marker: intraday low was the lowest since February 27, 2026 (before the war), still above the pre-war level of about 66 dollars
value.am_quote_basis: August futures at 06:39 GMT per AFP/Reuters
value.reversal_driver: a cargo ship was struck by an unknown projectile off the Omani coast, renewing Strait of Hormuz transit-safety concerns; the IRGC warned against a new shipping route
value.supply_backdrop: Oman opened temporary routes to ease tanker departures; Saudi tankers resumed toward Ras Tanura; the market is also eyeing a potential 2026 global supply surplus, with Iraq seeking a higher OPEC quota; US Cushing stocks are roughly 19 million barrels, below operational minimums

calculation.method: Trading Economics CFD day-over-day close for the headline level; intraday low and morning quotes from the AFP/Reuters wire

text: Crude oil staged an intraday reversal that flipped the macro narrative from the day before. In early trade Brent fell to a low of about 72.24 a barrel - its lowest since February 27, before the war, though still above the pre-war level near 66 dollars - with August futures down 1.44 percent at 72.68 and WTI off 1.08 percent at 69.58 by 06:39 GMT, weighed by rising regional supply as Oman opened temporary routes and Gulf tankers resumed. Prices then turned higher after a cargo ship was struck by an unknown projectile off the Omani coast, renewing Strait of Hormuz transit-safety concerns, and Brent closed up 1.35 percent at 74.74 (Trading Economics CFD). Even with the rebound, Brent is down about 23 percent over the past month. The bounce re-armed the oil-driven inflation risk that June 24's collapse had defused.

value.basis: Trading Economics ICE DXY, June 25 day-over-day
value.characterization: eased slightly, pausing a multi-session climb
value.context: the softer dollar accompanied the in-line monthly PCE and the renewed geopolitical bid in oil and gold

calculation.method: Trading Economics ICE DXY day-over-day level and percent change

text: The dollar paused its recent run. Trading Economics' ICE DXY basis put the index at 101.45 on June 25, down 0.16 percent on the day, snapping several sessions of gains; the dollar is still up 2.30 percent over the past month and 4.43 percent over twelve months. A softer dollar fit the day's two-sided tape - the in-line monthly PCE eased some of the hawkish pressure, while the renewed Strait of Hormuz risk premium drew bids into oil and gold rather than the dollar. The precise index level carries a vendor and snapshot basis and is held at medium confidence.

drivers: renewed Strait of Hormuz safe-haven bid
value.note: a modest rebound after a steep monthly decline

calculation.method: Trading Economics CFD day-over-day close and percent change

text: Gold rebounded modestly, rising 0.90 percent to 4,035.40 an ounce on June 25 (Trading Economics CFD), though it remains down about 10.5 percent over the past month and is up roughly 21 percent over the past year. The day's gain lines up with the softer dollar and the return of a Strait of Hormuz safe-haven bid - the same geopolitical impulse that reversed oil. These daily metal moves carry a vendor and spot/CFD basis and are held at medium confidence.

value.event: a commercial cargo ship was struck by an unknown projectile off the Omani coast on June 25, renewing Strait of Hormuz transit-safety concerns
value.iran_stance: the IRGC warned against a new Hormuz shipping route; parliament speaker Ghalibaf said Hormuz will never return to its pre-war status and floated charging transit fees, which US Secretary of State Marco Rubio dismissed
value.normalization_signs: Oman opened temporary routes on June 24 to ease tanker departures; a second vessel arrived since the April 24, 2026 Hormuz closure; Saudi tankers resumed toward the Ras Tanura terminal
value.strait_importance: about 20 million barrels per day of oil and petroleum products transited the strait in 2025 (US EIA)
value.market_role: the swing factor that reversed crude higher and revived the gold bid intraday

text: The geopolitical backdrop was the day's swing factor. After weeks of de-escalation that had normalized Gulf oil flows - Oman opening temporary routes, a second tanker arriving since the April 24 Strait of Hormuz closure, and Saudi cargoes resuming toward Ras Tanura - a cargo ship was struck by an unknown projectile off the Omani coast, renewing transit-safety fears in a corridor that carried about 20 million barrels a day of oil and products in 2025 (EIA). Iran's IRGC warned against a new shipping route, and parliament speaker Ghalibaf said Hormuz would never return to its pre-war status and floated transit fees, an idea US Secretary of State Marco Rubio dismissed. The renewed risk premium is what reversed oil higher and revived the gold bid; the precise market-impact attribution is carried at medium confidence.

value.core_tension: realized inflation confirmed firm (headline PCE 4.1 percent YoY, core 3.4 percent YoY) yet an in-line monthly print plus a renewed geopolitical risk premium produced a two-sided tape
what_rose: crude oil (Brent +1.35 percent to 74.74 after an intraday post-war low)
what_fell: Treasury yields modestly (10Y -1 bp to 4.40 percent, lowest since May 8)
value.unwind_note: a partial reversal of June 24's oil-collapse disinflation trade: the geopolitical risk premium came back into oil and gold
value.key_dependency: whether the Strait of Hormuz cargo-ship strike escalates or fades; a sustained disruption would re-arm the oil-and-inflation channel
value.policy_read: core PCE at 3.4 percent keeps the Fed's hawkish bias intact, even as the market read the monthly print as benign enough to rally bonds
value.caveats: FX and metals levels carry a vendor/snapshot basis and are held at medium confidence; the geopolitical market-impact attribution is medium

text: Net read: June 25 was a two-sided session that partly reversed the day before. The May PCE print confirmed firm realized inflation - headline 4.1 percent year over year, core 3.4 percent, well above target - but the monthly headline landed in line with expectations, so Treasury yields slipped (the 10-year to 4.40 percent, its lowest since May 8) and the 2s10s nudged to +31 bp. At the same time, the oil-collapse disinflation trade that defined June 24 partly unwound: a cargo-ship strike off Oman reintroduced a Strait of Hormuz risk premium, Brent reversed up 1.35 percent to 74.74 after an intraday post-war low, gold rose 0.90 percent, and the dollar eased 0.16 percent. The macro state now hinges on whether the Hormuz disruption escalates or fades - a sustained one would re-arm the oil-and-inflation channel and pressure a Fed whose core gauge is still running at 3.4 percent. FX and metals levels carry a vendor basis and the geopolitical impact attribution is held at medium confidence.
